exemplar.title: To what extent is Mentalization based Therapy (MBT) effective in treating symptoms of borderline personality disorder? (BPD)

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is Mentalization based Therapy (MBT) effective in treating symptoms of borderline personality disorder? (BPD)

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and effectively with clear and appropriate purpose and focus.
Research question is concise, specific, and consistently guides the essayâs structure.
weaknesses: Methodology is appropriate but implicitly described without an explicit search strategy section.
Minor breadth in focus when discussing SDT without specifying population or outcome measures.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall solidly in the top band (5â6). The topic and research question are exceptionally clear and consistently maintained, and the review never drifts from the guiding question. While the methodology demonstrates informed selection and a strong range of sources, it lacks a fully explicit description of search strategy and inclusion criteria. Given the overall excellence in focus and only minor methodological omissions, the full 6 marks are warranted.

strengths: Good grasp of MBT, attachment theory, and SDT, with generally relevant source application.
Accurate and consistent use of key psychological terminology.
weaknesses: Reliance on nonâpeerâreviewed websites for some statistics undermines credibility.
Coverage of neurobiological explanations and prevalence data is limited and could be expanded.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Subject knowledge and use of terminology are strong, but occasional reliance on general websites and gaps in theoretical coverage constrain the work to the upper end of the 3â4 band. Citations of âProfessional, n.d.â and mixed source quality prevent placement in the top band, justifying a score of 4.

strengths: Discussion synthesizes findings and proposes future research directions with logical, reasoned argumentation.
Research selection is clearly signâposted back to the research question, demonstrating consistent relevance.
weaknesses: Analysis sometimes stops at listing limitations without fully integrating them into a comparative synthesis.
Evaluation claims occasionally lack empirical backing or metaâanalytic weighting.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall critical thinking performance sits in the upper range of the 7â9 band. The student demonstrates clear, structured analysis and a reasoned discussion, highlighting strengths and limitations of studies and proposing future work. However, deeper synthesis of limitations and more rigorous backing for evaluative claims are needed to reach the top of the band. An 8 reflects strong but not flawless critical engagement.

strengths: Clearly appropriate structure meeting all extended essay conventions (title page, TOC, headings, conclusion).
Layout considerations (citations, numbering, formatting) are correctly applied overall.
weaknesses: Reference list shows inconsistent formatting styles, requiring standardization.
Statistical reporting formats vary and need uniformity in line with APA guidelines.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets the highest band (3â4) by fulfilling all structural and formatting conventions of the extended essay. Minor cosmetic and consistency issues do not detract from overall readability or organization, justifying the maximum 4 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Psychology terminologyâmentalization, attachment styles, RCT, global functioning, drop-out, SDT constructsâis used accurately and consistently.  Concepts are defined and then applied in context without major misuse.  Minor slips in phrasing (e.g., âiatrogenic effects due to loss of sense of selfâ) do not impede clarity.  The consistency and precision merit placement in the 5-6 band; a score of 5 recognises a few stylistic inconsistencies and occasional informal wording.

Formatting is generally consistent: in-text citations, italics, bold headings and a complete works-cited list.  Word count and section numbering are stated.  Page numbers appear in the delimiter markers, but some citation details (e.g., strange URLs, broken line breaks) deviate from a standard style guide.  Layout therefore meets most but not all criteria: awarding 3 within the 3-4 band reflects solid but not flawless formatting.

The essay demonstrates good grasp of MBT, attachment theory, SDT and key clinical concepts such as RCTs, symptom distress and drop-out rates.  Sources are academic and generally well-integrated, with correct study details and statistics.  Nevertheless, coverage of neurobiological explanations, prevalence data or opposite findings is limited, and some citations (e.g., âProfessional, n.d.â) are general websites rather than scholarly texts.  This meets the âgood knowledgeâ descriptor but only occasionally reaches excellence, so the upper mark of the 3-4 band (4) is appropriate.

The methodology is a structured literature review drawing on primary empirical papers, systematic table of contents, and a word-counted essay within the 4 000-word limit.  A diverse range of peer-reviewed RCTs, longitudinal and naturalistic studies is cited, showing informed selection.  Limitations are acknowledged (e.g., small sample sizes, lack of follow-ups).  However, the method section is implicit; there is no explicit description of search strategy or inclusion criteria, so the approach, while appropriate, is not fully âcompleteâ.  This places it solidly at 5 in the top band.

The student states the topic in the first line (âtherapeutic effectiveness of Mentalization-Based Therapy in treating BPDâ) and repeats it through clear section headings.  The introduction explicitly defines BPD, explains why treatment efficacy matters and outlines that the essay will evaluate MBT via attachment theory and SDT.  Throughout the body the focus never drifts from answering the guiding question, and the essay ends with an explicit conclusion that returns to the purpose.  These features match the descriptor of âcommunicated accurately and effectively with clear and appropriate purpose and focus,â justifying the top band and the full 6 marks within it.

The student selects eight empirical studies directly related to MBT and BPD, covering adult, adolescent and dual-diagnosis populations.  Each studyâs design, sample size and key findings are summarised.  Relevance is consistently sign-posted back to the RQ.  However, the search appears limited to English-language Western studies and omits systematic justifications for inclusion.  Consequently, the work aligns with the lower end of the 7-9 descriptor, warranting 7 marks.

The research questionââTo what extent is Mentalization-Based Therapy (MBT) effective in treating symptoms of borderline personality disorder?ââis concise, specific and fully aligned to the subsequent literature review.  All major sections (studies 1â4, alternative theory, discussion) are organised around answering this question.  Minor weakness: the wording could specify population (adolescents vs adults) or outcome measures, so the focus is occasionally broad when discussing SDT.  This slight breadth warrants 5 rather than 6, but the work is still clearly in the 5-6 band.

The essay follows EE conventions: title page with word count, structured table of contents, logical headings, introductionâbodyâdiscussionâconclusion flow and a reference list.  All required elements are present and in an appropriate order, so it meets the highest band; minor issues such as uneven heading styles are cosmetic, thus 4 marks.

The discussion section synthesises findings, addresses heterogeneity of BPD symptoms, cultural issues and practical challenges for therapists, then proposes future research directions.  Arguments are logical and structured, demonstrating critical evaluation of MBTâs limitations and potential.  Nonetheless, evaluative claims sometimes lack empirical backing (e.g., suggestion that integrated therapy is âpreferredâ), and no meta-analytic weighting is attempted.  These weaknesses position the work at the lower end of the 7-9 band (score 7).

For each study the student identifies strengths (e.g., RCT design) and limitations (small samples, lack of follow-up), and links statistical outcomes to therapeutic claims.  The analysis goes beyond description, yet it often stops at listing limitations without integrating them into a comparative synthesis or weighting evidence quantitatively.  This matches the top of the 4-6 bandâclear, mostly supported analysisâbut falls short of the depth and sophistication required for 7-9.

annotations.comment: The final sentence in the introduction is truncated and disrupts readability. Ensure each paragraph concludes with a complete thought.
The inclusion of a precise title clearly stating the topic ensures immediate orientation for the reader and meets the formatting requirements.
The research question is concise, specific, and consistently guides the essayâs structure, demonstrating strong alignment to the inquiry focus.
The word count (3928 words) stays under the 4000âword limit, satisfying the formatting requirement and demonstrating adherence to guidelines.
The table of contents is detailed with accurate page references, guiding readers effectively through the reportâs structure.
The table of contents lacks an explicit methodology section; consider adding a clear âMethodologyâ heading to reflect your search strategy and inclusion criteria.
Multiple statistics in the introduction cite âProfessional, n.d.â, a nonâpeer-reviewed source. Replace with current epidemiological studies to strengthen credibility.
The definition of mentalization is clearly presented with an authoritative citation, demonstrating accurate use of psychological terminology.
The section is overly descriptive and fails to connect attachment theory to MBTâs practical application, weakening its relevance to the RQ.
Integrate attachment theory insights with MBT interventions to clarify how theoretical constructs inform treatment steps.
The description of MBTâs focus and clinical implementation is precise and correctly cites Bateman & Fonagy (2010).
The section remains descriptive without critiquing how diminished mentalization capacity directly affects MBTâs therapeutic mechanisms.
Link the theoretical concept of mentalization more explicitly to empirical MBT studies in order to tighten the focus on the research question.
The attachment theory discussion provides a thorough historical overview, demonstrating strong subject knowledge and context.
Terms like âiatrogenic effectsâ appear without definition or example; define specialized terms to enhance reader understanding.
Provide clinical vignettes or case examples illustrating adverse iatrogenic effects to ground terminology in practice.
The reference list displays inconsistent formatting styles and mixed source types; standardize according to APA or another single convention.
Study 1 is wellâdescribed, including sample sizes and dropout rates, showing strong engagement with empirical details.
The analysis omits specific p-values or effect sizes; including these would substantiate claims of significant improvements.
The RCT design is correctly highlighted as a strength in Study 2, demonstrating awareness of methodological rigor.
Statistical reporting is inconsistent (e.g., âM = 0.04â vs incomplete metrics). Apply a uniform APA reporting style.
Study 3 reports p-values and psychometric findings appropriately, reinforcing analytic depth.
The small sample size (n=16) is noted but without discussion of statistical power; consider addressing implications for validity.
Study 4âs use of multiple standardized instruments for adolescents is commendable and shows methodological breadth.
Overreliance on self-report measures risks bias; triangulate with clinician-rated assessments for more robust conclusions.
Introducing SDT expands the theoretical framework, showing intellectual initiative in exploring alternative explanations.
This section remains theoretical without integrating empirical SDT findings; connect theory directly to study outcomes.
Define all acronyms on first use (e.g., ETCM) to ensure clarity for readers unfamiliar with specialized terms.
The RCT design in the SDT study is properly acknowledged, reflecting good methodological understanding.
Discussion points about cultural factors lack accompanying empirical citations; add specific studies to support these claims.
The discussion synthesizes heterogeneity and diagnostic challenges effectively, demonstrating critical evaluation of MBTâs robustness.
The concluding paragraph summarizes findings but omits a concise reflection on methodological limitations; include this for completeness.
Several citations rely on non-peer-reviewed websites (e.g., MentalHelp.net); replace with primary scholarly articles to strengthen source validity.
